subject: A Review of Set Theory
question: Use the previous problem to show that $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable.
answer: By the previous problem there is no onto map from $\mathbb{N}$ to $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ so that $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable.
explanation: Answer: By the previous problem there is no onto map from $\mathbb{N}$ to $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ so that $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable.
